items.title: ILAE 2017 Classification
items.contentHtml:
ILAE Classification 2017

  • Focal seizures: onset in one hemisphere; subclassify into with consciousness (previously simple) or with altered consciousness (previously complex).
  • Generalized seizures: bilateral involvement from the beginning; include tonic-clonic, absences (typical vs atypical), myoclonic, clonic, tonic and atonic.
  • Unknown: focal or generalized onset cannot be determined.
  • Focal seizures with evolution to bilateral tonic-clonic: formerly called secondarily generalized.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis of syncope vs epilepsy
items.title: Syncope vs epilepsy: keys
items.contentHtml:
Syncope vs epilepsy

  • Syncope: prodrome of dizziness, nausea, sweating; quick recovery; brief myoclonic movements (<15 sec) may occur but are not epileptic.
  • Epileptic seizures: aura (e.g. epigastric, déjà vu), lateral tongue biting, urinary incontinence, postictal confusion prolonged (minutes to hours).
  • Serum prolactin: elevated after tonic-clonic seizure (not in syncope), but it is not routine.
  • EEG: epileptiform abnormalities support epilepsy.

items.title: First line focal crises
items.contentHtml:
First-line focal seizures

  • Lamotrigine or levetiracetam are first-line options in adults with focal seizures (lower profile of adverse effects and teratogenicity).
  • Carbamazepine and phenytoin are effective but with more interactions and side effects; in the elderly, avoid carbamazepine due to the risk of hyponatremia.
  • Valproic acid is an alternative, but avoid it in women of childbearing age due to teratogenicity and risk of polycystic ovary syndrome.
  • Start monotherapy; If it fails, rotate to another drug or add a second.

items.foco: Status epilepticus: definition and critical time
items.title: Definition and critical time
items.contentHtml:
Status epilepticus: definition and critical time

  • Operational definition: continuous seizure >5 min or repeated seizures without recovery of consciousness between them.
  • Critical time: after 5 minutes treatment is started; If it does not respond to first line (benzodiazepines) within 20-30 min it is considered established status; at 60 min refractory status.
  • Neural damage: after 30-60 min of continuous activity there is a risk of irreversible injury.
  • Temporal classification: early status (0-5 min), established (5-30 min), refractory (>60 min).

items.title: Initial treatment status
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment of status epilepticus

  • Step 1: ABC stabilization (airway, breathing, circulation), oxygen, monitoring, capillary glucose.
  • Step 2: Benzodiazepine IV/IM/buccal/rectal: lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg/dose) or diazepam 0.2 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg) or midazolam IM 0.2 mg/kg (max 10 mg) if there is no IV access.
  • Step 3: If it continues >5 min after first dose, repeat benzodiazepine once.
  • Step 4: If it persists, start phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV or fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg PE (loading dose).

items.title: Benzodiazepines: choice and dosage
items.contentHtml:
Benzodiazepines in status epilepticus

  • Lorazepam (0.1 mg/kg IV, max 4 mg/dose) is preferred over diazepam due to its longer half-life in the CNS (12-24 h) and less redistribution.
  • Diazepam (0.2 mg/kg IV, max 10 mg) has rapid entry into the brain but is redistributed, with recurrence in 15-30 min.
  • Midazolam IM (0.2 mg/kg, max 10 mg) is an alternative if there is no IV access; also intranasal or buccal (0.2-0.3 mg/kg).
  • Adverse effects: respiratory depression and hypotension; monitor.

items.title: Phenytoin/fosphenytoin: use
items.contentHtml:
Phenytoin and fosphenytoin in status

  • Phenytoin: loading dose 20 mg/kg IV (max 50 mg/min) diluted in SF; risk of hypotension and arrhythmias (QT prolongation).
  • Fosphenytoin: prodrug, dose in phenytoin equivalents (PE) 20 mg/kg PE IV/IM; faster administration (150 mg PE/min) and lower risk of phlebitis.
  • Both have non-linear kinetics; monitor levels (total and free in hypoalbuminemia).
  • Contraindicated in high-grade AV block, severe bradycardia.

items.title: Causes of refractory status
items.contentHtml:
Causes of refractory status epilepticus

  • Encephalitis (viral, autoimmune), brain tumor, CVA (ischemic or hemorrhagic), traumatic brain injury.
  • Metabolic disorders: hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, hypocalcemia, uremia, hepatic encephalopathy.
  • Alcoholic withdrawal or benzodiazepines/barbiturates.
  • CNS infections: meningitis, brain abscess.
  • Autoimmune diseases: encephalitis due to anti-NMDA antibodies, anti-LGI1.
  • If there is no apparent cause, rule out status no. convulsive.

items.title: EEG in non-convulsive status
items.contentHtml:
EEG in nonconvulsive status epilepticus

  • Definition: continuous epileptic activity on EEG (>30 min) without prominent motor manifestations, or recurrent seizures without recovery of consciousness.
  • Key EEG finding: generalized or focal epileptiform discharges (spikes, sharp waves, complexes spike-wave) at >2.5 Hz; or pattern of PLEDs (periodic lateralized discharges) with clinical correlation.

items.title: Febrile seizures: criteria
items.contentHtml:
Febrile seizures: criteria and management

  • Definition: seizure associated with fever (>38°C) in children from 6 months to 5 years, without CNS infection or acute electrolyte alteration.
  • Simple febrile seizure: generalized, <15 min, single in 24 hours, without recurrence.
  • Seizures complex febrile: focal, >15 min, multiple in 24 h, or with postictal deficit.
  • Management: treat fever (antipyretics do not prevent recurrence); if active crisis >5 min: rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg or buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg.
  • EEG or routine neuroimaging are not indicated in simple cases; in complex consideration.
  • Do not start routine prophylactic antiepileptics.

items.title: Epilepsy and pregnancy
items.contentHtml:
Epilepsy in pregnancy: safe drugs

  • Lamotrigine and levetiracetam are the drugs of choice due to lower teratogenicity (risk of malformations <3%).
  • Valproic acid is contraindicated due to high risk of neural tube defects and neurodevelopmental disorders (low IQ).
  • Phenytoin and carbamazepine associated with facial clefts and cardiopathies.
  • Monitor lamotrigine levels (they decrease during pregnancy) and adjust doses.
  • Supplement with folic acid 5 mg/day from preconception.

items.title: SUDEP: factors and prevention
items.contentHtml:
Sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP)

  • Definition: non-traumatic death, not due to status epilepticus, in a patient with epilepsy, without identified structural or toxic cause.
  • Risk factors: frequent generalized tonic-clonic seizures, non-compliance with therapy, polycomorbidity, male sex, early onset of epilepsy.
  • Mechanism: probable postictal cardiorespiratory depression (central apnea, bradycardia, hypoxemia).
  • Prevention: optimize adherence to antiepileptics, avoid alcohol, nighttime monitoring (seizure detection devices), epilepsy surgery in refractory cases.
